one_line_takeaway: A large-scale SoK-style study across 19 obfuscation techniques, 15 LLMs, and 2 coding agents finds that code obfuscation against LLM-based vulnerability detectors is a double-edged sword -- virtualization and mixed-language transforms are the strongest evasion techniques (~80%/~75% attack success on C/C++), an 8B-parameter robustness boundary and a reasoning-vs-robustness trade-off exist across model families, and while coding agents detect vulnerabilities better overall, they inherit backbone-model weaknesses and suffer 'partial understanding' when hot-plugged with an unofficial backbone.
key_finding: Seven numbered findings, each grounded in the paper's own tables/figures: (1) obfuscation produces a dual effect -- expected downgrades coexist with unexpected upgrades (e.g., on Smart-bugs, D4+D5+D6+D7 and C3 sometimes beat the unobfuscated baseline for qn-32b and lm-70b; on ReposVul-py, L2/L3 give modest gains for DeepSeek/OpenAI models), and the aggregate count of successes/failures stays roughly stable pre- vs post-obfuscation. (2) Virtualization-based obfuscation (C3) is the strongest attack, reaching an ~80% attack success rate on the C/C++ datasets; mixed-programming-language obfuscation (L7/L8) is second at ~75%. (3) Model robustness shows an 8B-parameter boundary: sub-8B models are markedly more fragile, gains above 8B are diminishing, and reasoning-augmented models (DeepSeek-R1-Distill variants, o3-mini) score higher on clean code but are paradoxically more downgrade-prone under obfuscation, especially within the Qwen and Llama families; by model family, DeepSeek shows the lowest stability, then Llama, then OpenAI, then Qwen (most stable). (4) Under the stricter 'type evaluation' (correct existence+type+location, matching Table 6), obfuscation yields mixed up/downgrades, but under the looser 'existence evaluation' (Table 13, Appendix) success rates rise by roughly 40% on average and obfuscation rarely hurts (e.g., Qwen-14B on PrimeVul jumps from 0.39 original to 0.80 after L1) -- meaning models can often still flag 'something is wrong' but lose the ability to pinpoint type/location. (5) Dataset/vulnerability effects: memory-safety and access-control CWEs (CWE-787, 125, 119, 120, 416, 476) dominate both downgrade and upgrade lists; simplifying obfuscation (flattening, removing redundancy) hurts detection more than complexity-adding obfuscation; by language, downgrade rate is highest for Smart-bugs/Solidity (2475/23040 = 10.7%), then ReposVul-cpp (9.2%), PrimeVul/C (8.4%), ReposVul-py (7.5%), with a parallel ranking for upgrade rate (Smart-bugs 8.1% > PrimeVul 7.1% > ReposVul-py 6.7% > ReposVul-cpp 6.4%). (6) Coding agents (Copilot, Codex) beat general-purpose LLMs on detection success both before and after obfuscation on Smart-bugs, ReposVul-cpp, and PrimeVul (Tables 8/10, appendix), but their relative downgrade/upgrade sensitivity to obfuscation is 'almost identical' to standalone LLMs, with L7 (inline assembly) and C3 (virtualization) remaining the most effective attacks against agents too (Table 9/11, appendix). (7) A controlled hot-plugging experiment (ds-v3 vs. copilot-gpt-5 vs. copilot-ds-v3) on Python and C++ shows copilot-gpt-5's weaker Python performance stems from the GPT-5 backbone itself, not the Copilot framework (ds-v3 ~ copilot-ds-v3 > copilot-gpt-5, with 4 wins/3 losses/6 ties for ds-v3 vs. copilot-ds-v3 across obfuscations); but the unofficial copilot-ds-v3 hybrid shows a 'partial understanding' failure mode -- e.g., on 165_admin.py under L4+L5+L6 it detects the layout transform but misses the CSV-injection vulnerability that both native ds-v3 and the official copilot-gpt-5 catch, and a parallel case (17_comment.cpp under L7) recurs on C++.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is a systematization-of-knowledge (SoK) plus a large empirical study of how source-code obfuscation affects LLM-based vulnerability detection. It (1) builds a taxonomy of 19 concrete obfuscation techniques grouped into 11 subcategories and 3 classes (layout, data flow, control flow), collapsed for experiments into 10 obfuscation 'combos' (L1, L2, L3, L4+L5+L6, L7, L8, D1+D2+D3, D4+D5+D6+D7, C1, C2, C3, plus D8); (2) implements these transformations, using gpt-4o as the obfuscation engine, across four languages (Solidity, C, C++, Python) drawn from four vulnerability benchmarks; (3) evaluates 15 LLMs across 4 families (Qwen, LLaMA/DeepSeek-R1-Distill variants, DeepSeek, OpenAI) at parameter scales from 7B to 671B, plus two coding agents (GitHub Copilot/GPT-5, Codex/GPT-5-Codex); and (4) introduces a 4-level (1-4) detection scoring scheme that separately captures whether a vulnerability's existence, type, and location are correctly identified, defining 'downgrade' (3/4->1/2 after obfuscation) and 'upgrade' (1/2->3/4 after obfuscation) as its core outcome metrics.
threat_model: The threat model is source-level, semantics-preserving code obfuscation used by an adversary (e.g., a malicious code contributor or an attacker trying to slip a vulnerable file past an automated LLM-based auditor) to evade LLM-based vulnerability detectors and coding agents, without altering the program's externally observable behavior. The obfuscator (a GPT-4o-driven transformation pipeline) is assumed to have white-box access to the source file; the victim is a downstream LLM or coding agent performing static vulnerability detection on that (possibly obfuscated) file. The paper treats both directions of the effect (evasion/downgrade and the unexpected detection-improving 'upgrade') as security-relevant, since 'upgrade' has defensive implications (e.g., using obfuscated variants to denoise misleading cues) while 'downgrade' is a concrete evasion capability.
limitations: The study is explicitly self-reported as SoK/empirical rather than proposing a new defense: obfuscation itself is generated by a single LLM (gpt-4o), which could bias which transformations are 'representative' of each technique class; the scoring rubric (1-4 scale keyed to exact ground-truth match S={a}) is coarse and does not assess partial/near-miss explanations beyond the 4 buckets; sample sizes per dataset/CWE were deliberately capped (max 5 vulnerable files per CWE for C/C++/Python) to control imbalance, which limits statistical power per vulnerability type; RQ5's coding-agent evaluation is restricted to a small top-20-per-dataset subset (5 downgrade + 5 upgrade cases x 4 datasets) rather than the full corpus, and covers only two agents (Copilot, Codex), both GPT-5-family-backed except for the ad hoc copilot-ds-v3 hybrid; the hot-plugging root-cause analysis in Finding #7 is based on a handful of concrete file-level anecdotes (165_admin.py, 17_comment.cpp) rather than a large controlled sample. The paper is a study/benchmark paper, not a new detector or defense -- it proposes future directions (obfuscation-aware training, cross-layer reasoning, precision-preserving hot-plugging) but does not implement or evaluate any of them.

datasets_used: Smart-bugs (Solidity smart contracts, 9 vulnerability types, avg 128 LOC, avg 35 functions)
PrimeVul (C, 119 vulnerability types, avg 143 LOC, avg 256 functions)
ReposVul cpp (C++, 35 vulnerability types, avg 84 LOC, avg 172 functions)
ReposVul py (Python, 89 vulnerability types, avg 210 LOC, avg 223 functions)
quantitative_results: Virtualization obfuscation (C3) attack success rate ~80% on C/C++ datasets
Existence-evaluation success rates rise ~40% on average vs. type-evaluation (e.g., Qwen-14B on PrimeVul: 0.39 original -> 0.80 after L1 under existence evaluation)
Downgrade rate by language: Smart-bugs/Solidity 10.7% (2475/23040 cases), ReposVul-cpp 9.2%, PrimeVul/C 8.4%, ReposVul-py 7.5%
Upgrade rate by language: Smart-bugs 8.1%, PrimeVul 7.1%, ReposVul-py 6.7%, ReposVul-cpp 6.4%

Original (unobfuscated) detection success rate on Smart-bugs ranges from 0.29 (ds-qn-7b) to 0.89 (o3-mini); on PrimeVul from 0.07 (ds-qn-14b) to 0.29 (ds-r1); on ReposVul-cpp from 0.12 (qn-32b) to 0.29 (ds-v3); on ReposVul-py from 0.11 (qn-7b) to 0.27 (gpt-4o)
copilot-ds-v3 vs. ds-v3 on Python obfuscations: 4 wins, 3 losses, 6 ties (in favor of ds-v3)
~20% relative performance drop of copilot-ds-v3 vs. ds-v3 on Python under L3, L4+L5+L6, D1+D2+D3, C1, traced to a single file (165_admin.py)
baselines_compared: 15 LLMs across 4 families: Qwen2.5-Instruct (7B/14B/32B) and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen (7B/14B/32B); Llama-3.1/3.3-Instruct (8B/70B) and DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Llama (8B/70B); DeepSeek-V3 and DeepSeek-R1 (671B); GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4o, o3-mini
2 coding agents: GitHub Copilot (backed by GPT-5) and Codex (backed by GPT-5-Codex)
Ablation/control condition: copilot-ds-v3 (Copilot agent framework with DeepSeek-V3 backbone swapped in) used to isolate framework effects from backbone-model effects
